# Frequently asked questions {#sec-faq}

## Is the calibrated stack an independent validation of the per-PV workflow?

No --- and the package is careful never to claim it is. Pipeline C is
*calibrated to* the Pipeline A target $(\bar\beta_{\mathrm{MI}},
T_{\mathrm{MI}})$ through the affine map of @eq-ccc, so their agreement is a
statement that the calibration did its job, not that two independent methods
concur. The scientific content is the **computational saving** --- one MCMC fit
in place of $M$ --- justified by the point identity (@eq-identity) and the
moment algebra (@eq-ccc-moments), not by the agreement itself.

## Why are the Korea Pipeline B estimates blank?

Because the reweighting gate of @eq-gate fails for Korea: all ten plausible
values have Pareto-$\hat k \ge 0.7$ (@fig-pisa-khat). The diagnostics remain
fully visible, but the numerical reweighted estimates are returned as missing
with `status = "computed_but_withheld"`. Blank cells are the *intended* output;
the test suite makes any accidental promotion a hard failure. The United States,
whose ten $\hat k$ all fall below $0.7$, is reported normally.

## Which degrees of freedom reproduce Table 4?

Classic-Rubin degrees of freedom. The archived Pipeline A objects record
`df_method = "classic"`, so their endpoints are classic-Rubin results even
though the manuscript text describes them as Barnard--Rubin intervals. The
Barnard--Rubin option exists in `code/01_core/rubin_pool.R` but is not
substituted after the fact; because the degrees of freedom are large, the two
conventions coincide to the two decimals Table 4 prints (@sec-perpv, @sec-repro).

## Why is the frequentist leg called "REML" when it is fit by ML?

The manuscript and its exhibits label the per-PV frequentist comparator
*per-PV REML*, but the archived producer fits it with maximum likelihood
(`REML = FALSE`). This package ships the archived ML numbers and records the
naming difference rather than relabeling the evidence or refitting it. At these
sample sizes the fixed-effect comparison is unaffected; the point is disclosure,
not correction (@sec-sim-legs).

## Why do I see both 23.20 and 23.58?

They are different calculations that happen to be numerically close. The value
$23.20$ is the model-based Pipeline A/C difference between the Korean and U.S.
between-school slopes ($92.94 - 69.74$). The value $23.58$ comes from a legacy,
design-based level-1 contrast that uses only the final weight. They travel
through different covariance and target routes, so they should not be treated as
the same estimator; the weighting-sensitivity contrast that surfaces them is a
secondary robustness result (@sec-pisa-reversal).

## Is the "Korea variance share is twice the U.S." sentence checked?

No, and that is deliberate. No archived producer supports the statement, and the
most direct intraclass-correlation calculation does not reproduce the "roughly
twice" wording, so the claim is registered as an `unverified` manuscript
statement rather than reverse-engineered into a supporting number (@sec-repro).

## Do I need to rerun the 4,000 simulations or refit PISA?

No. The `quick` track rebuilds every table and figure from the audited combined
evidence in minutes, with CRAN packages only. Use `simulation --mode smoke` to
exercise the computational path, and reserve the full `simulation` or `pisa`
paths (which need CmdStan, and, for PISA, authorized local data) for when a
complete new estimation run is genuinely your goal (@sec-setup).

## Can I run anything useful without the PISA microdata?

Yes --- most of the package. The `quick`, `intermediate`, `verify`, and
`pisa --mode cached` tracks use only released, sanitized evidence and never read
a unit record. Only `pisa --mode full --execute` needs authorized local OECD
data (@sec-pisa-data).

## Why might a regenerated figure PDF have a different hash?

Font embedding and PDF metadata vary across systems, so an identical figure can
produce different bytes. Scientific parity is therefore checked from plot data,
table values, normalized text, and explicit numeric anchors --- never from PDF
bytes --- and the visual-QA report separates benign rendering differences from
content changes (@sec-repro).

## Where is the deeper analysis?

This guide shows you *how* to reproduce the results. The scientific argument ---
*what* the study found and *why* each modeling choice was made --- is the
manuscript [@lee2026onemcmc]. The general-purpose implementation of the workflow
is the companion R package
[**pvstackr**](https://github.com/joonho112/pvstackr); this repository ships the
frozen engine that produced the paper's specific results (@sec-software-boundary).
